content.text: multiple state updates triggered in the same event are grouped into a single re-render
call, React collects all the updates, applies them together, and renders exactly once with the resulting state.

Without batching, a handler that calls three setters would cause three sequential renders — each time the component function runs, builds JSX, and reconciles the DOM. With batching, the same handler causes one render with all three updates already applied.
Batching is invisible from the outside. The component never sees intermediate states — it only sees the final combined snapshot. This is a correctness guarantee, not just a performance hint.

attrs.alt: Diagram comparing unbatched (3 renders) vs batched (1 render) state updates

content.text: The scope of automatic batching changed significantly between React 17 and React 18.
Not batched — one render per setter
as part of the concurrent renderer. Every context that transitions to React's scheduler — including async code — is now batched by default. The only way to opt out is with
fires once — not three times. All three setters schedule updates into the same queue. React processes the queue after the event handler returns and runs one render with
How React Processes the Update Queue
When a setter is called, React does not immediately re-render. Instead, it enqueues the update onto the component's fiber and schedules work:
setA(1) is recorded as 'set a to 1' in the update queue for this component.
setB(2) and setC(3) are enqueued similarly. The handler runs to completion.
After the handler finishes, React processes all queued updates in order, producing the new state values.
React calls the component function once with the fully-resolved new state — a=1, b=2, c=3 simultaneously.
React commits the resulting DOM changes in one pass.

forces React to flush all pending updates synchronously before returning. Use it when you need the DOM to reflect a state change before reading layout — for example, to measure a newly rendered element.
flushSync hurts performance. It bypasses batching and forces a synchronous layout. Only use it when you genuinely need a DOM measurement immediately after a state change.
) work correctly with batching because they receive the queued intermediate value, not the snapshot:

When React processes the update queue, it applies functional updaters in sequence, each receiving the result of the previous one. Snapshot-based updates all read the same frozen value and overwrite each other.
Mixing Updater Forms in One Handler

React processes the queue in order. Literal values replace; functional updaters transform the previous queued value. A literal update after a functional one discards the functional result, so order matters.
Within a single event handler's synchronous flow, the component function is
re-invoked. It only sees the final state after the queued updates produce a render. However, intermediate renders occur in a few special cases:
Forces a synchronous render between the flushSync call and the next line of code.
Two clicks on two separate buttons trigger two separate render cycles — they are never batched together.
A setState call inside useLayoutEffect triggers a synchronous render before the browser paint.
Updates from different createRoot() trees are never batched across each other.
In React 18's concurrent renderer, batching interacts with priority. Updates inside
are low-priority and may be superseded by urgent updates:
update is urgent and renders immediately. The
update is a transition — React may skip intermediate values and only render SearchResults with the latest query, achieving a smoother typing experience.
Class components have the same batching behavior.
inside React event handlers is batched, and the class-style functional updater also composes through the queue:
If your component renders more times than expected, check whether batching is working. Common causes of missing batching:
Each setter inside setTimeout or .then() triggers its own render. Migrate to React 18 or restructure state to update in fewer calls.
Wrapping each setter in its own flushSync forces three separate synchronous renders — defeating the purpose.
State updates in different createRoot() trees are never batched together.
In development, React 18 calls render functions twice to detect side effects. This looks like double renders but is not extra state updates — it does not affect production.

Batching makes React efficient by collapsing multiple state updates from the same event into one render. React 18 extends automatic batching to all contexts, including setTimeout and async code. Functional updaters are the safe default when you need to compose updates within a single handler — they chain through the queue rather than reading a frozen snapshot. For the rare case where you need a DOM read between updates,
opts out of batching at a performance cost.
Batching applies whether you update primitive state or object/array state. One common pattern is to consolidate multiple related fields into a single
object to reduce the number of setters you need:
Consolidating tightly coupled state into one object is often clearer than relying on batching to hide multiple updates. However, splitting unrelated state into separate
calls is the React recommended pattern — don't consolidate everything.
A single user action can trigger state updates in
— for example, a parent and a child both have event handlers, or a context update affects many subscribers. React batches all of these together:

React processes all updates from a single event — regardless of which component they originated from — in one batch. The component tree re-renders once with all updates applied, not once per component.
React Testing Library wraps interactions in
, which flushes all pending state updates after the interaction. This ensures that after a test action, the DOM reflects the batched result:
, state updates may not be flushed, causing
to see stale DOM. Always wrap state-triggering test actions in
(RTL does this automatically for its user-event helpers).
A common question is: why is it safe to batch updates? What if the second setter depends on a side effect of the first render? React's answer is the
React components must be pure functions of their inputs (props + state). They must not produce observable side effects during rendering.

Because rendering is pure, skipping intermediate renders is always safe — the component will produce the same output given the same final state, regardless of how many intermediate snapshots were skipped.
Side effects belong in effects (useEffect, useLayoutEffect), which run after the render and commit. React guarantees effects run after the full batch is committed, not between individual updates.
If your component breaks when intermediate renders are skipped, it likely has an impure render — a side effect in the component body. Fix the impurity rather than working around batching.

The React DevTools Profiler shows batched updates as a single commit bar. Each bar in the flame graph represents one synchronous render cycle — one batch flush. If you see N bars for N setState calls triggered by one user action, batching is not working.
= batching not working. Check for React 17 async context, multiple roots, or flushSync usage.
= StrictMode double-invocation (intentional, not a bug, does not affect production).

Key takeaways: Multiple setters in one handler produce one render (React 18: everywhere; React 17: only in React event handlers). Functional updaters chain through the queue; snapshot reads see the pre-handler state. flushSync opts out of batching at a performance cost. Batching is invisible to the component — it only sees the final combined snapshot.
